incorporations.original: prolonged the agony of the Republic by a full month
incorporations.replacement: prolonged the agony of the Republic by a month
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'di un mese' means simply 'by a month'; the primary draft's 'full' was an unjustified addition

incorporations.original: which will shortly witness the fall of Luciano Manara, gasping his last breath
incorporations.replacement: which will shortly witness Luciano Manara fall gasping
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'vedrà cadere boccheggiante Luciano Manara' places the verb and participle together; the Gemini draft's more direct syntax ('Manara fall, gasping') better reflects this, and the final tightens it further by dropping the comma

incorporations.replacement: Courage — it is the final trial!
incorporations.reason: The Gemini draft's structure 'Courage, it is the final test' better conveys the Italian exclamatory rhythm 'Coraggio è l'ultima prova!'; the word 'trial' (neither draft's choice) was preferred as it captures both the sense of ordeal and of testing, fitting the battlefield context more precisely than 'proof' or 'test'

incorporations.original: The self-styled agent of the good Lord
incorporations.replacement: The self-styled procurator of the good Lord
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'procuratore' specifically means procurator (one who acts on another's authority), not the vaguer 'agent'; Gemini's 'procurator' is the precise and period-appropriate term

The Italian 'gemito estremo' means 'last moan/groan', not 'sigh'; the primary draft's 'sigh' softened the original's anguish. 'Moan' (from neither draft — Gemini had 'groan') was chosen as closer to the Italian 'gemito' while less guttural than 'groan'; 'last' for 'estremo' follows Gemini

edgren_influences.note: Edgren defines feretro strictly as 'bier' (the frame on which a corpse is carried), confirming this over Gemini's 'casket'. A modern translator might default to 'coffin' or 'casket', but 'bier' is both more precise and more period-appropriate.

Edgren confirms palpebra as 'eyelid'. The Italian 'batter palpebra' (beat an eyelid) is an idiom for blinking; the literal rendering 'let fall an eyelid' preserves the physical image that Crespi chose, whereas Gemini's 'blink' loses the bodily concreteness.

Edgren lists 'parole' as a distinct sense of parola. In the court-martial context ('non avendo io ancora data la mia parola'), the military meaning — a prisoner's formal pledge not to escape — is exactly right. Gemini's 'word' is technically accurate but misses the precise military-legal term that the context demands.
